what_it_does: A standalone guard script that exercises FsStore session methods (save_session, load_session, list_sessions) against a real temporary directory, verifying the methods exist, records round-trip correctly, mutations persist, missing reads return None, and a fresh FsStore instance can read what the first instance wrote.
observations: Uses a hand-rolled check() harness with global PASS/ok counters instead of a test framework
Verifies method existence with callable(getattr(...)) before behavioral tests
Tests fail-soft behavior: load_session('nope') must return None, not raise
Checks exact list ordering from list_sessions()
Re-instantiates FsStore with the same root to prove persistence is on disk, not in-memory
Cleans up the temp directory in a finally block
standards: The 'fresh store instance' durability check is the central guard against silently dropping real persistence
The session record shape mirrors what a review/walkthrough session would write
Fail-soft missing-id read is explicitly tested
conventions: Docstring explains the file's purpose, linked ticket T3-SESSION-CHURN, and run command
Uses sys.path.insert to make the company package importable
Manual PASS/FAIL print harness with a numeric counter
concerns: Global mutable state PASS and ok inside check() makes parallel execution unsafe and is unusual for a test
Assumes list_sessions() returns lexicographically sorted stems; a different ordering strategy would fail
Hardcodes a specific session schema; changes to session shape could make the guard stale
No use of an assertion framework, so failure output is just a printed line
notes: A standalone regression guard for the on-disk session persistence contract of FsStore, runnable without pytest.
questions: Does FsStore.list_sessions() guarantee sorted output, or is the test relying on filesystem order?
Should the guard also exercise delete/remove_session if such a method exists?
Is the session schema used here the canonical schema, or should it be generated from a fixture?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the implementation: it guards the session storage capability with real-disk round-trip and durability checks.
intention_signals: The file is trying to make the session-storage API too expensive to accidentally remove by encoding its behavioral contract in an executable, filesystem-backed guard.
novelty: It is a self-contained, runnable guard rather than a conventional unit test, explicitly framed as protection against 'silent capability loss' in future build loops.
contribution: Provides an executable behavioral contract for FsStore session persistence that can be run standalone and that documents the expected round-trip, listing, mutation, and fail-soft semantics.
summary_for_embedding: A standalone filesystem-backed guard that verifies FsStore session save/load/list methods persist records across fresh store instances and fail softly on missing ids.
intention_for_embedding: To prevent future changes from silently removing or breaking the real-disk session persistence API used by review and walkthrough sessions.

symbols.does: Evaluates a boolean condition, updates global pass/fail counters, and prints a PASS/FAIL line.
symbols.description: The check function is the manual assertion harness for this guard. It mutates global variables PASS and ok to track how many checks passed and whether any failed, then prints a labeled result line for each condition.

symbols.does: Creates a temporary FsStore root, runs a sequence of checks on session persistence, then cleans up the temp directory.
symbols.description: The main function sets up a real temporary directory, instantiates FsStore, and exercises save_session, load_session, and list_sessions through existence checks, empty-store behavior, round-trip preservation, mutation persistence, multi-session coexistence, and a fresh-instance durability check before removing the temp directory in a finally block.
